proposition: Accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, foreclosing Kettering's CFAA claim.
cited_case: Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021)
retrieved_case: Van Buren v. United States
brief_sentence: The alleged Computer Fraud and Abuse Act claim is foreclosed by Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021) and Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020), because Defendant Collier had accessed the information with valid credentials and at Kettering's direction during a system outage.
finding_analysis: Van Buren holds that one does not exceed authorized access under the CFAA when using valid credentials to obtain information he is entitled to access (reversing the conviction despite an improper purpose), directly supporting the proposition that accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the CFAA.

proposition: HIPAA provides no private right of action.
cited_case: Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008)
retrieved_case: Wilson v. Collins
brief_sentence: HIPAA provides no private right of action, and the materials at issue are de-identified by subject codes, not “PHI.” See Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008); 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(2).
finding_analysis: Wilson v. Collins is a constitutional challenge to Ohio's DNA collection statute (Fourth/Fifth Amendment and due process) and says nothing about HIPAA or any HIPAA private right of action, so it addresses a completely different topic and does not support the proposition.

finding_analysis: The Sixth Circuit in Stolle expressly held that the OUTSA displaces overlapping common-law tort claims (tortious interference and conspiracy) that merely restate the operative facts of a trade-secret-misappropriation claim, directly supporting the proposition.

proposition: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.

finding_analysis: Iqbal expressly states that to survive a motion to dismiss a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face, directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

proposition: Communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.

proposition: Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.
cited_case: United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999)

quoted_text: ["Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat."]

brief_sentence: “Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.” United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999). Thus, advising Kettering of Ms. Collier's statutory obligations cannot constitute extortion.
finding_analysis: Jackson holds a reputation-injury threat is extortion under 18 U.S.C. 875(d) when the threatener has no plausible claim of right (here, threatening tabloid disclosure of paternity to extort money from Cosby); it does not support the proposition that informing a party you will report misconduct to authorities is lawful, and the supporting quote is fabricated.

proposition: Ohio's public policy protections shield reporting of misconduct to regulators and sponsors as protected conduct.
cited_case: Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc., 78 Ohio St.3d 134, 150 (1997)
retrieved_case: Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc.

brief_sentence: Reporting misconduct to regulators and sponsors is protected conduct: Whistleblower Protection Act, 5 U.S.C. § 2302; False Claims Act retaliation provisions, 31 U.S.C. § 3730(h); Ohio's public policy protections (Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc., 78 Ohio St.3d 134, 150 (1997)).
finding_analysis: Kulch supports that Ohio public policy protects an at-will employee's reporting of workplace violations to a regulator (OSHA) via a wrongful-discharge tort, but the proposition overstates and generalizes the holding (e.g., adds reporting to "sponsors," which the case never addresses, and omits the statute's strict-compliance limits).

proposition: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion.

finding_analysis: Flatley holds the opposite of the proposition: it found that Mauro's threats to expose/report Flatley's alleged misconduct unless paid constituted criminal extortion as a matter of law, and that such threats are extortionate even when the victim actually committed the crime or indiscretion.

proposition: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion.

proposition: The Supreme Court construes “exceeds authorized access” narrowly to cover obtaining information from areas of a computer to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials.
cited_case: Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021)

brief_sentence: The Supreme Court construes “exceeds authorized access” narrowly to cover obtaining information from particular areas of a computer—such as files, folders, or databases—to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials. Van Buren, 593 U.S. at 380-86.
finding_analysis: Van Buren holds that one exceeds authorized access only by obtaining information from areas of a computer that are off-limits, not by misusing information accessible with valid credentials, directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

brief_sentence: HIPAA does not create a private right of action. Wilson, 517 F.3d at 429.
finding_analysis: This opinion concerns the constitutionality of Ohio's DNA collection statute under the Fourth, Fifth, and Fourteenth Amendments and never mentions HIPAA or any private right of action under it, so it does not support the proposition.

proposition: Tortious-interference and conversion theories are preempted by OUTSA where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation.
cited_case: Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 918-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011)
retrieved_case: Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Office Products, LLC
brief_sentence: Its tortious-interference and conversion theories are preempted by OUTSA where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation. Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Office Depot, 821 F. Supp. 2d at 918-23.
finding_analysis: The opinion supports OUTSA preemption of tortious-interference and conversion/unjust-enrichment theories only under a partial 'same facts' standard, preempting just the portions resting on misappropriation while expressly preserving claims with an independent factual basis, so the proposition's categorical framing overstates the qualified holding.

proposition: Any defamation-based claim arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged.
retrieved_case: Hecht v. Levin
syllabus: Torts—Defamation—Attorneys at law—Complaint filed with grievance committee of local bar association is part of a judicial proceeding—Statement made in course of attorney disciplinary proceeding enjoys an absolute privilege against a civil action based thereon, when—R.C. 2305.28 does not apply to attorney disciplinary proceedings.
brief_sentence: Any defamation-based claim arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged. Hecht, 66 Ohio St. 3d at 460-61.
finding_analysis: Hecht v. Levin holds litigation-related defamatory statements are absolutely privileged only when they bear some reasonable relation to the proceeding, so the unqualified "any defamation-based claim" framing overstates the holding by omitting that relevance limitation.
